common_name: Blue Butterfly Bush (Sky Flower Clerodendrum, Uganda Clerodendrum)

planting.note: Plant container-grown shrubs after last frost in partial shade to full sun; in zone 9, Blue Butterfly Bush performs best in morning sun with afternoon shade protection in summer. It blooms most profusely in spring and fall when temperatures are moderate. In zone 9a, it will freeze back to the roots in hard winters but resprouts vigorously; in zone 9b-10 it is semi-evergreen to evergreen. Prune back hard after each major bloom flush to stimulate repeat flowering. Can also be grown in a large container.

directions: Plant in full sun to partial shade in well-drained, fertile soil; Blue Butterfly Bush adapts to most soils and responds well to regular fertilization and moderate watering during active growth periods. Clerodendrum ugandense (now sometimes reclassified as Rotheca myricoides) is a large shrub from the highlands of East Africa — Uganda, Kenya, Tanzania — producing one of the most charming and distinctive small flowers in the tropical plant world. Each individual flower consists of four pale blue petals spreading like wings and a single deep violet-blue lower petal curving downward, creating the unmistakable silhouette of a small blue butterfly perched on the stem. The flowers are borne in loose, airy panicles at the branch tips throughout the warm season in zone 9, with the most spectacular flushes in spring and fall. The overall effect of a plant in full bloom — dozens of tiny sky-blue butterflies hovering in clusters at every stem tip — is extraordinary and unlike any other flowering shrub available in zone 9. Blue Butterfly Bush is a fast grower that responds well to hard pruning, which stimulates vigorous new growth and repeat blooming cycles. Excellent as a container specimen on a sunny to partly-shaded patio.
sources: University of Florida IFAS Extension — Clerodendrum ugandense (Blue Butterfly Bush)
Floridata Plant Encyclopedia — Clerodendrum ugandense
LSU AgCenter — Tropical Flowering Shrubs for Louisiana Landscapes
Texas A&M AgriLife Extension — Clerodendrum species for Gulf Coast

planting.note: Plant container-grown specimens after last frost in partial shade to full sun; Fireworks Clerodendrum blooms primarily in late winter to early spring (December–March in zone 9) when it produces its most spectacular floral display. In zone 9a it freezes to the roots in hard winters but resprouts vigorously in spring; in zone 9b-10 it is evergreen. The plant spreads by root suckers and can colonize a large area over time — manage by removing suckers or installing a root barrier. The dark burgundy undersides of the leaves are as ornamental as the flowers.

directions: Plant in full sun to partial shade in well-drained, fertile soil; Fireworks Clerodendrum is a vigorous grower that can reach 6–15 feet quickly in zone 9-11, making it best suited for large spaces or where a bold statement is desired. Clerodendrum quadriloculare is a large tropical shrub or small tree native to the Philippines, Sulawesi, and New Guinea, widely grown in tropical and subtropical landscapes for its spectacular winter-to-spring floral display: large, domed to rounded panicles up to 12 inches across of individual tubular flowers, each with a long pink-to-deep-rose tube opening into 5 white to pale pink star-shaped petal lobes that spread flat — the overall effect of hundreds of these flowers clustered together in a dome against the dark foliage is precisely that of an exploding fireworks burst, earning the common name. The large leaves have a distinctive two-toned appearance — dark, glossy bronze-green above and deep burgundy-purple below — making the foliage ornamental even when the plant is not in bloom. Blooming primarily December–March in zone 9, the plant provides floral color at a time when relatively few tropical shrubs are in bloom. The suckering habit can be used to create a large informal screen or hedge; remove excess suckers to maintain a single-stemmed specimen form.
sources: University of Florida IFAS Extension — Clerodendrum quadriloculare (Fireworks Clerodendrum)
Fairchild Tropical Botanic Garden — Clerodendrum quadriloculare
Floridata Plant Encyclopedia — Clerodendrum quadriloculare
LSU AgCenter — Winter-Blooming Tropical Shrubs for Gulf Coast Louisiana

planting.note: Plant container-grown or bare-root trees in late spring in full sun with very well-drained, deep soil; Marula is highly drought-tolerant once established but intolerant of waterlogged soils. Marula is dioecious — separate male and female trees are required for fruit production; purchase sexed (grafted) stock from specialty nurseries or plant 2–3 seedlings and hope for mixed sexes. Established trees in zone 9b have survived brief dips to 26–28°F. Young trees require frost protection for the first 2–3 winters.

directions: Plant in full sun in deep, well-drained soil of any type — Marula is remarkably adaptable to poor, sandy, or rocky soils and intensely drought-tolerant, thriving in conditions that challenge most fruit trees. Sclerocarya birrea subsp. caffra (Marula) is a large, spreading, deciduous African fruit tree native to the woodlands and savannas from Ethiopia and Nigeria south to South Africa, famous throughout sub-Saharan Africa as one of the most important traditional food and beverage trees of the continent. The plum-sized fruits (1–1.5 inches diameter) ripen in December–March in South Africa; in zone 9 they ripen in late summer–fall. When ripe, the thin, pale yellow skin covers soft, extremely juicy, intensely aromatic flesh with a rich, complex flavor combining tropical fruit notes reminiscent of mango, pineapple, and lychee — prized fresh, fermented into a traditional beer, and distilled into the popular Amarula Cream liqueur (produced commercially in South Africa). The large, brown, extremely hard seeds inside each fruit contain edible kernels with a rich, nutty flavor and high oil content used in traditional African cosmetics. Elephants travel great distances to feed on fallen marula fruits, giving rise to the folklore that they become inebriated — a myth, though the fruits do ferment rapidly when fallen. Marula is increasingly grown as an edible landscape tree in zone 9b–11.
sources: University of Florida IFAS Extension — Tropical Fruit Trees for South Florida: Sclerocarya
California Rare Fruit Growers — Marula profile
USDA National Agroforestry Center — Sclerocarya birrea agroforestry uses
Morton, J.F. (1987) Fruits of Warm Climates — Sclerocarya birrea

common_name: Biriba (Wild Sweetsop, Rollinia, Lemon Meringue Fruit)

planting.note: Plant container-grown trees in late spring when soil is warm; Rollinia is frost-tender and should be protected from temperatures below 32°F — it is strictly a zone 10–11 outdoor tree but can be grown as a large container specimen moved indoors during frost events in zone 9. It grows rapidly from seed and can fruit in 2–4 years. Plant in partial shade to full sun with well-drained, fertile, moist soil. The fruits must be harvested before they fall and consumed immediately as they deteriorate extremely rapidly — the flesh bruises and ferments within hours of harvest or impact.

directions: Plant in full sun to partial shade in rich, moist, well-drained soil; Biriba grows rapidly in zone 10–11 humid conditions and benefits from regular irrigation and fertilization to produce its heavy crops of large, distinctive fruits. Rollinia deliciosa (often treated as a synonym within the soursop genus: Annona mucosa) is a tropical fruit tree from the Amazon basin of Brazil, Colombia, and Peru, prized by tropical fruit enthusiasts for producing one of the most extraordinarily flavored and unusual-looking fruits in the Annona family. The fruits are large (4–8 inches), covered in soft, flexible, yellowish-green to golden spines or fingerlike projections that soften completely when ripe, and contain white, extremely juicy, sweet-tart flesh described as tasting like a combination of lemon meringue, vanilla custard, and banana — some connoisseurs consider it the finest-flavored of all the Annona relatives, surpassing cherimoya and soursop. The fruits ripen very rapidly (within 1–2 days of picking) and bruise at the slightest touch, making commercial distribution virtually impossible and confining the fruit almost entirely to home gardens and local markets in tropical growing regions. In zone 10–11 South Florida, Biriba is increasingly grown by rare-fruit enthusiasts and community gardens as a fast-growing, productive tree that rewards the patient gardener with extraordinary fruits unavailable in any market.
sources: University of Florida IFAS Extension — Rollinia deliciosa (Biriba) for South Florida
California Rare Fruit Growers — Rollinia deliciosa profile
Purdue University Center for New Crops — Rollinia mucosa (Biriba)
Morton, J.F. (1987) Fruits of Warm Climates — Rollinia deliciosa

planting.note: Plant container-grown trees in late spring in partial shade to full sun with consistently moist, humid, fertile soil; Langsat is strictly tropical and frost-tender, suitable only for zone 10b–11 outdoor planting or for large containers in zone 10a. It is one of the most shade-tolerant of tropical fruit trees and actually benefits from some overhead protection when young. Very slow to reach bearing age from seed — grafted trees produce fruit in 4–6 years. The tree prefers high humidity and consistent moisture similar to its humid lowland forest native habitat.

directions: Plant in partial shade to full sun in consistently moist, humus-rich, well-drained soil; Langsat is more shade-tolerant than most tropical fruit trees and performs well with overhead canopy protection when young, mimicking its native understory forest environment. Lansium parasiticum (formerly L. domesticum) is a medium tropical tree native to the Malay Peninsula, the Philippines, and Indonesia, where it is one of the most beloved native fruits — at once common enough to be sold from roadside carts throughout Southeast Asia and exotic enough to be a premium market item in Japan and specialty stores. The fruits are produced in long, grape-like clusters directly on the main trunk and branches (cauliflory), each fruit a small (1–2 inch) oval, pale yellowish-tan berry with thin, papery skin. Inside are 3–5 segments of translucent, juicy, sweet-tart flesh with a flavor variously described as combining grape, grapefruit, longan, and rose water — extraordinarily refreshing in tropical heat. The 'Duku' form (sometimes listed separately) produces larger, thicker-skinned, sweeter fruits than the standard Langsat; 'Longkong' is a selected clone from Thailand with very sweet, thin-skinned fruits highly valued in Southeast Asian markets. Virtually unavailable commercially outside Southeast Asia and rare specialty markets, Langsat is a coveted addition to zone 11 South Florida tropical fruit gardens.
sources: University of Florida IFAS Extension — Lansium parasiticum (Langsat)
California Rare Fruit Growers — Langsat/Duku profile
Purdue University Center for New Crops — Lansium domesticum (Langsat)
PROSEA — Plant Resources of Southeast Asia: Lansium parasiticum
